subConcepts.name: When the Shared Letter Is a Vowel

learningGoal: Spot when the hidden letter is a vowel (A, E, I, O, U)
Why vowels are sneaky — they fit lots of words, so you need to test carefully

screens.title: Could a vowel be hiding in there?
screens.body:
Daisy is working on shared-letter puzzles after school.

Did you know? Each pair is really **two words joined together** — the missing letter **ends** the first word and **starts** the second. So TE(?)RM becomes TEA + ARM when the letter is A.

Sometimes the shared letter is a **vowel** — A, E, I, O or U. Let's find out!

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Only 5 to try!
screens.body: Look at the pairs **TE(?)RM and PE(?)ND**. Here's the great thing about vowels — there are only **5 to try**: A, E, I, O, U. That's a short list you can whizz through!

steps.why: Only A makes real words for both sides: TEA + ARM. TEE+ERM? TEI+IRM? TEO+ORM? None work!

steps.text: Try each vowel: A? E? I? O? U?
steps.why: Only 5 options — try them all!

steps.text: Answer: A → TEA + ARM, PEA + AND
steps.why: The only vowel that makes real words for ALL pairs ✓

screens.title: Your turn — which vowel fits?
screens.body: Which vowel completes ALL the word pairs?

interaction.question: Which vowel completes ALL the word pairs?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The letter A completes both pairs: PA + AGE, and SPA + ACE. A is hiding in both — test every vowel to be sure! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is A. The letter A completes both pairs: PA + AGE, and SPA + ACE. A is hiding in both — test every vowel to be sure! ✓

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: You've got vowels sorted!
screens.body: When you think the answer might be a vowel, here's your game plan:

steps.text: 1. There are only 5 vowels: A, E, I, O, U
steps.why: That's a short list to check!

steps.text: 2. Try each one in the restrictive word first
steps.why: Which vowel makes a real word?

steps.text: 3. Test your answer on ALL pairs
steps.why: The vowel must work everywhere, not just one pair ✓

subConcepts.name: When the Shared Letter Is a Consonant

learningGoal: Track down a shared consonant hiding between word pairs
Why the restrictive-word trick is your best friend with consonants

screens.title: Hunt the hidden consonant!
screens.body:
Evie is working through her VR practice book.

Remember: each pair is **two words joined together**. The missing letter **ends** the first word and **starts** the second.

Most shared-letter answers are **consonants** — that's 21 letters! But don't worry, the restrictive-word trick means you only need to test a few.

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: The smart shortcut
screens.body: Look at **CU(?)AP and FA(?)ALL**. You definitely don't need to try all 21 consonants! Find the restrictive word (the pair where fewest letters work) — it usually narrows things down to just 2 or 3 options.

steps.why: CUT+TAP works. CUP+PAP? CUB+BAP? Only T gives clean common words for both pairs.
CUT + TAP — both real words!
Works for ALL pairs — that's the answer!

screens.title: Your turn — find the consonant!
screens.body: Which consonant completes ALL the word pairs?

interaction.question: Which consonant completes ALL the word pairs?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! The letter T completes both pairs: HOT + TOOL, and WIT + TUNE. T is a very common shared consonant! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is T. The letter T completes both pairs: HOT + TOOL, and WIT + TUNE. T is a very common shared consonant! ✓

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Consonants — no sweat!
screens.body: When the shared letter is a consonant, here's your plan:

steps.text: 1. Don't panic — you won't need to try all 21 consonants
steps.why: The restrictive word limits your options

steps.text: 2. Find the word pair with fewest possible completions
steps.why: Which pair only works with 2-3 letters?

steps.text: 3. Test each candidate on ALL pairs
steps.why: One letter must work everywhere ✓

subConcepts.name: Two Word Pairs — The Standard Format

learningGoal: Solve the most common shared-letter format quickly and confidently
Find the one letter that completes BOTH word pairs

screens.title: One letter, two word pairs!
screens.body:
Maisie is warming up with standard shared-letter questions.

This is the format you'll see most often in the test: find **one letter** that completes **both** pairs of words. The letter ends one word and starts the next. Let's crack it!

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: The 3-step method
screens.body: Let's work through **CA(?)EN and BU(?)ANK** together. This 3-step method makes it really straightforward:

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Start restrictive, find a candidate, then verify. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — always start with the restrictive pair first!

steps.why: BUS+SANK? BUT+TANK? BUN+NANK? Only T and S make clean words, but CAS isn't a word — only T works for both pairs.
This makes real words!
Both pairs work — done!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which letter completes **both** word pairs?

interaction.question: Which letter completes BOTH word pairs?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The letter N completes both pairs: PAN + NICE, and WIN + NEST. Both are clean, common words! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is N. The letter N completes both pairs: PAN + NICE, and WIN + NEST. Both are clean, common words! ✓

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: You've nailed the method!
screens.body: For standard 2-pair questions, just follow these steps:

steps.why: Which pair has fewer possible letters?

steps.text: 2. List letters that work for that pair
steps.why: Usually only 2-3 letters make real words

steps.text: 3. Test each candidate on the other pair
steps.why: The shared letter must work for BOTH ✓

learningGoal: Build speed solving 2-pair shared-letter questions
Get confident with the restrictive-word method so it becomes second nature

screens.title: Two pairs — spot the link!
screens.body:
Evie is doing extra practice before her test.

You know the drill — one letter goes in both gaps. It **ends** the first word and **starts** the second word in each pair. Can you find it?

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Start with the trickier pair
screens.body: For **HO(?)EN and STO(?)ARK**, the pair with fewer possible letters is your best starting point. Solve that one first, then check the other.

steps.why: HOP+PEN and HOT+TEN both work for pair 1, but STOT+TARK? Not real! Only P works for both.
HOP + PEN
Confirmed!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which letter completes **both** word pairs?

interaction.question: Which letter completes BOTH word pairs?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The letter P completes both pairs: CUP + POOL, and SHOP + PALE. Both sides make common words! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is P. The letter P completes both pairs: CUP + POOL, and SHOP + PALE. Both sides make common words! ✓

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: You're getting fast at this!
screens.body: Quick checklist for 2-pair questions:

steps.text: Which pair limits the options most?
steps.why: Start there — fewer letters to test

steps.text: Does the letter make REAL words both sides?
steps.why: Both the ending and starting word must exist

steps.text: Does it work for BOTH pairs?
steps.why: A letter that only works for one pair is wrong ✓

learningGoal: Tackle 3-word shared-letter questions with confidence
Discover why more words actually makes things EASIER, not harder

screens.title: Three words — actually easier!
screens.body:
Daisy is attempting the harder shared-letter questions.

Did you know? Three words might look scarier, but they're actually your **friend**! The **same letter** goes at the start of all three words — and more words means **more chances to eliminate** wrong letters.

Let's find the one letter that makes ALL of them real words!

visualProps.label: One letter completes all three words:

screens.title: More words = faster elimination
screens.body: Here's why three words is actually brilliant: for **_AIN, _ULE, _OAD**, a wrong letter will usually fail on at least one word. That makes ruling out wrong answers much quicker!

steps.why: Only MULE, RULE and YULE are common words — just 3 options to test!
All three are real words!
No other letter manages all three

screens.title: Your turn — all three words!
screens.body: Which letter completes **all three** words?

interaction.question: Which letter completes ALL THREE words?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The letter M completes all three: MOON, MAIL, MAST. L gives LOON, LAIL — LAIL isn't a word! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is M. The letter M completes all three: MOON, MAIL, MAST. L gives LOON, LAIL — LAIL isn't a word! ✓

visualProps.label: One letter completes all three words:

screens.title: Three words — your secret advantage!
screens.body: Next time you see 3 words, smile — you've got this:

steps.why: Wrong letters fail faster with 3 words than 2

steps.text: Still use the restrictive word first
steps.why: Start with the word that has fewest possible letters

steps.text: Test your answer on ALL words before moving on
steps.why: Don't skip the check — it catches mistakes ✓

learningGoal: Quickly spot which word pair narrows down your options the most
Learn why unusual letter patterns are your best clue — they're the most restrictive

screens.title: The one that narrows it down
screens.body:
Daisy is learning to spot the restrictive word quickly.

Remember: each pair is **two words joined**. The missing letter **ends** the first and **starts** the second.

Here's the time-saving trick top scorers use: find the **restrictive word** — the pair where only a few letters could possibly work. It's like having a shortcut built right into the question!

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Spot the clues!
screens.body: In **STA(?)ING and FEA(?)OOF**, look for unusual patterns — double letters, rare letter combos, or long words with few completions. These are your tell-tale signs!

interaction.sentence: The ____ word is the pair where fewest letters make real words on both sides
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! The restrictive word limits your options — always start there. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — the restrictive word is the pair where fewest letters work!

steps.why: How many letters make a word ending in OOF? ROOF, HOOF, POOF, WOOF — very few! This is the restrictive pair.

steps.text: Only R makes real words for this pair
steps.why: STAR + RING
Confirmed on the other pair!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Find the restrictive pair, then solve!

interaction.question: Which letter completes ALL the word pairs?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The letter P completes both pairs: SHEEP + PAIR, and DEEP + POOL. The longer fragments make it easier to spot! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is P. The letter P completes both pairs: SHEEP + PAIR, and DEEP + POOL. The longer fragments make it easier to spot! ✓

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Your restrictive-word radar is on!
screens.body: Look for these clues to find the restrictive word quickly:

steps.why: These have very few possible completions
Fewer words start or end this way
CRAS_ has fewer options than CA_ ✓

learningGoal: See how fragment length changes the puzzle — and use it to your advantage
Discover why longer fragments actually make things easier for you

screens.title: Short gap or long gap?
screens.body:
Evie is exploring how letter positions change the puzzle.

Here's something really useful: sometimes the letter sits between short fragments like CA(?)EN. Sometimes it's between longer ones like STOR(?)ICE. The longer the fragments, the **fewer** letters can work — which makes your job easier!

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Longer = easier!
screens.body: In **CA(?)EN and BU(?)ANK**, longer fragments make the puzzle **easier** — fewer letters can possibly fit. Shorter fragments give more options, so check both sides.

steps.why: Two letters before and two after — the letter sits in the middle. CA_ makes many words (CAB, CAD, CAR, CAT...) so check the other side too.
Real words on both sides!
Works everywhere!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which letter fills the gap?

interaction.question: Which letter completes ALL the word pairs?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! The letter R completes both pairs: WAR + RINK, and FAR + ROOT. Short fragments give more options — check both sides carefully! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is R. The letter R completes both pairs: WAR + RINK, and FAR + ROOT. Short fragments give more options — check both sides carefully! ✓

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Now you know the shortcut!
screens.body: Use fragment length to your advantage:

steps.why: You'll need to test more options
The answer almost gives itself away!

steps.text: Always start with the LONGEST fragment pair
steps.why: That's your most restrictive word ✓

learningGoal: Use elimination like a detective — rule out wrong letters one by one
Save time by testing each option against just ONE pair first

screens.title: Be a letter detective!
screens.body:
Daisy is using elimination to solve a tricky puzzle.

Remember: each pair is **two words joined**. The missing letter **ends** the first and **starts** the second.

Options: **D**  |  **N**  |  **R**  |  **S**  |  **T**

Let's play detective — go through each option one by one. Most will fail to make real words, so you can cross them off!

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Eliminate against ONE pair first
screens.body:
Pick one pair and try all 5 options. Cross off any that don't make real words. Then test what's left on the other pair.

Options: **D**  |  **N**  |  **R**  |  **S**  |  **T**

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Eliminate, then verify the survivor. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by testing letters against one pair first!

steps.why: Try each option: GLAN+NONE? GLAR+RONE? GLAS+SONE? GLAT+TONE? GLAD+DONE ✓ — only D makes real words!
This letter makes real words!
The last letter standing wins!

screens.title: Your turn — eliminate!
screens.body:
Try each option. Which letter survives?

Options: **D**  |  **N**  |  **R**  |  **S**  |  **T**

interaction.question: Which letter completes ALL the word pairs?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! The letter D completes both pairs: GLAD + DONE, and MILD + DAMP. Go through each option: N gives GLAN (not a word), R gives GLAR (not a word). Only D works! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is D. The letter D completes both pairs: GLAD + DONE, and MILD + DAMP. Go through each option: N gives GLAN (not a word), R gives GLAR (not a word). Only D works! ✓

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Elimination — your backup plan!
screens.body: When you're stuck, elimination always works:

steps.text: 1. Pick one pair (the more restrictive one)
steps.why: More letters will fail here
Does it make a real word on BOTH sides?

steps.text: 3. Test survivors on the other pair(s)
steps.why: The last letter standing is your answer ✓

subConcepts.name: Tricky Pairs — When Multiple Letters Seem to Work

learningGoal: Handle the sneakiest questions — where multiple letters seem to work
Learn to avoid the classic trap of assuming too early

screens.title: Watch out — sneaky trap ahead!
screens.body:
Daisy is tackling a question where two letters seem right.

Remember: each pair is **two words joined**. The missing letter **ends** the first and **starts** the second.

Did you know? Some questions are designed to **trick** you on purpose! A letter might work perfectly for one pair but **fail** on the other. That's why checking **every** pair is so important!

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Never assume — always verify!
screens.body: For **SAN(?)ARE and FIN(?)EAR**, the most common mistake is finding a letter that works for ONE pair and assuming it works for all. Always test both!

steps.why: SAND+DARE ✓. SANK+KARE? No. What about SANT+TARE? TARE is a word, but FINT isn't!
But does it work for the other pair too?
Yes! Both pairs confirmed — that's the answer!

screens.title: Your turn — avoid the trap!
screens.body: Which letter works for **ALL** pairs, not just one?

interaction.question: Which letter completes ALL the word pairs?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The letter D completes both pairs: SAND + DARE, and FIND + DEAR. T tempts with TARE but FINT isn't a word! Always check ALL pairs. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is D. The letter D completes both pairs: SAND + DARE, and FIND + DEAR. T tempts with TARE but FINT isn't a word! Always check ALL pairs. ✓

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: You won't fall for the trap now!
screens.body: Here's how to stay one step ahead of the question setters:

steps.text: NEVER stop after checking one pair
steps.why: A letter that works for one pair might fail on others

steps.text: Test your answer on EVERY pair
steps.why: Both words must be real in EVERY pair

steps.text: If two letters seem to work, one will fail on a pair you haven't checked
steps.why: The test setters design it this way on purpose! ✓

learningGoal: Spot the mistakes others make when multiple letters seem to fit
Catch yourself before picking a tempting wrong answer

screens.title: Why is "P" wrong?
screens.body:
Evie is checking her practice test answers.

The answer chosen was **P** — She tried P first: CLAP + PELT — both real words! But then TRIP + POON — POON isn't a standard word.

Can you spot why it fails and find the correct letter?

visualProps.label: Wrong answer: P — why doesn't it work?

screens.title: The verification step catches mistakes!
screens.body:
She tried P first: CLAP + PELT — both real words! But then TRIP + POON — POON isn't a standard word.

This is why you must **always test on ALL pairs** before choosing your answer.

steps.text: Wrong: P — fails on one pair
steps.why: She tried P first: CLAP + PELT — both real words! But then TRIP + POON — POON isn't a standard word.
Real words on both sides
Works for EVERY pair!

screens.title: Find the correct letter!
screens.body: Which letter actually works for **all** pairs?

interaction.question: Which letter completes ALL the word pairs?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! The letter M completes both pairs: CLAM + MELT, and TRIM + MOON. P works for CLAP+PELT but fails on TRIP+POON! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is M. The letter M completes both pairs: CLAM + MELT, and TRIM + MOON. P works for CLAP+PELT but fails on TRIP+POON! ✓

visualProps.label: One letter ends the first word and starts the second:

screens.title: Learn from mistakes!
screens.body: The most common shared-letter mistakes:

steps.text: Picking a letter that works for ONE pair only
steps.why: Always check EVERY pair before answering

steps.text: Forgetting to check BOTH words in a pair
steps.why: The letter must make real words on BOTH sides

steps.text: Rushing — take 5 extra seconds to verify
steps.why: A quick check saves you from silly mistakes ✓
